name: Owner Role Clarity & Delegation
purpose: Defines the boundary between the owner's strategic role and the manager's operational role, establishing clear delegation frameworks so the owner can focus on business growth and leadership rather than being permanently trapped on the shop floor.

detailed_objectives: Audit how the owner currently spends their time and identify tasks that should be delegated
Define the owner's role in terms of strategic responsibilities versus operational duties
Create a delegation framework specifying which decisions and tasks belong to the manager, shift leads, and individual team members
Build the manager's confidence and competence to operate independently without constant owner oversight
Establish clear escalation criteria so the owner is only called upon for genuinely strategic or exceptional issues
secondary_objectives: Reduce the owner's working hours on the shop floor to create time for business development
Empower the team by trusting them with meaningful responsibility and decision-making authority
Create a business that can operate profitably when the owner is not physically present

why_this_matters: The most common trap in independent cafe ownership is the inability to stop being an operator and start being a leader. Many cafe owners work 50 to 70 hours per week, spending the vast majority of that time making coffee, serving customers, cleaning, and handling the same tasks as their team — but with the added burden of dealing with suppliers, accounts, and maintenance in whatever time is left. This is unsustainable. It leads to burnout, poor decision-making, strained relationships, and a business that cannot survive the owner's absence for even a single day. The root cause is usually a failure to delegate, driven by a combination of perfectionism ('nobody does it as well as I do'), fear ('what if they make a mistake that costs me money'), and habit ('I have always done it this way'). Delegation is not abdication — it is the deliberate transfer of appropriate authority and responsibility to capable team members, with clear expectations and accountability. The Chartered Management Institute identifies effective delegation as one of the core competencies separating successful business leaders from overwhelmed operators. In a cafe context, this means the owner should be spending their time on activities that only the owner can do: setting the strategic direction, managing key relationships (landlord, bank, major suppliers), financial oversight, and developing the team and culture. Everything else — rota building, stock ordering, shift management, customer complaint resolution, daily cleaning oversight — should be delegated to the manager and shift leads. The transition requires structured support: clear role definitions, documented handover of responsibilities, and patience while the team develops the confidence to operate without constant oversight.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Conduct an honest time audit. For one full week, the owner tracks how they spend every 30-minute block — on the bar, on admin, in meetings, dealing with suppliers, managing staff, cleaning, or doing strategic work. At the end of the week, categorise each block as 'only I can do this' or 'someone else could do this.' Most owners discover that 60-80% of their time is spent on tasks others could handle. Week 2: Identify the three tasks that consume the most owner time and could be delegated immediately. Common candidates: rota building, stock ordering, and social media posting. For each task, select the team member best placed to take it on, spend one session showing them how you do it, give them a written brief of expectations, and let them do it for two weeks while you resist the urge to interfere. Review after two weeks — correct any issues but do not take the task back. Create a simple Role Clarity Document: two columns showing 'Owner responsibilities' (strategy, finance, key relationships, culture, team development) and 'Manager/team responsibilities' (daily operations, shift management, customer complaints, cleaning, stock ordering). Share this with the team so everyone understands the new structure.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a comprehensive Delegation Matrix mapping every operational function to a specific role and person. Use four delegation levels: Level 1 (Do it and tell me afterwards), Level 2 (Do it and tell me about any issues), Level 3 (Recommend a decision and I will approve), Level 4 (Refer to me — I decide). Most daily operational tasks should be at Level 1 or 2. Only genuinely strategic or high-risk decisions should be at Level 3 or 4. Create an Escalation Protocol defining what constitutes a genuine escalation versus what should be handled independently. Examples: a customer complaint about a cold drink is Level 1 (handle it). A customer threatening legal action is Level 4 (escalate immediately). A stock delivery arriving short is Level 2 (handle it, inform the owner). A key supplier relationship breakdown is Level 4 (owner involvement needed). Build the manager's strategic capability by including them in supplier negotiations, financial reviews, and business planning conversations. Delegation fails when the delegate has no context — give them enough information to make good decisions independently.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The owner's time should now be predominantly strategic. Track the owner's time split monthly: target at least 50% of working hours on strategic activities (business development, financial management, culture building, external relationships) rather than floor operations. Conduct a quarterly delegation review — are the delegated tasks being handled effectively? Does the delegate need more support or training? Are there additional tasks that should be delegated? Introduce the owner to the concept of 'working on the business rather than in the business' through industry networks, business coaching, or peer groups like the Federation of Small Businesses or local cafe owner networks. Create a succession mindset — if the owner were to sell the business in three years, would it operate independently? This future-proofing exercise drives delegation discipline even for owners who never intend to sell. Celebrate successful delegation: when the team handles a difficult situation independently that the owner would previously have managed, acknowledge it publicly as evidence of growth.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Conduct a one-week time audit tracking all owner activities in 30-minute blocks
Categorise each time block as 'only I can do this' or 'someone else could do this'
Identify the top three tasks for immediate delegation
Select delegates, brief them, and hand over with clear written expectations
Create a Role Clarity Document showing owner versus manager/team responsibilities
Share the Role Clarity Document with the entire team
Commit to not taking delegated tasks back — correct issues through coaching, not reclamation

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a comprehensive Delegation Matrix mapping every function to a role and delegation level
Create an Escalation Protocol defining what genuinely needs owner involvement
Include the manager in supplier negotiations and financial reviews to build context
Document all delegated tasks with written expectations, authority limits, and review points
Train the manager on decision-making frameworks for independent problem-solving
Establish a weekly owner-manager alignment meeting to review delegation effectiveness
Build a decision log where the manager records key decisions made independently

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track owner time split monthly (target: 50%+ strategic, not operational)
Conduct quarterly delegation reviews with the manager
Join industry networks or peer groups for owner-level strategic development
Create a succession mindset by stress-testing the business's owner-independence
Celebrate successful independent decision-making by the team
Review the Delegation Matrix annually as the business evolves

deliverables: Owner Time Audit template (one-week tracking sheet)
Role Clarity Document (owner vs manager/team responsibilities)
Delegation Matrix (all functions, roles, delegation levels)
Escalation Protocol (what to escalate, what to handle independently)

tools.description: One-week tracking sheet where the owner records how they spend every 30-minute block, categorised by activity type, with a final analysis showing the split between owner-only and delegatable tasks.
Two-column document clearly separating owner strategic responsibilities from manager and team operational responsibilities, shared with the entire team for transparency.
Comprehensive grid mapping every operational function to a specific role and delegation level (Do and report, Do and flag issues, Recommend for approval, Refer for decision).
Clear guide defining what constitutes a genuine owner escalation versus what should be handled independently by the manager or shift lead, with worked examples from cafe operations.

Monthly tracker monitoring the owner's time split between strategic and operational activities, with targets, trend analysis, and identification of tasks that keep pulling the owner back to the floor.

failure_modes: Owner delegates tasks but then micromanages execution, undermining the delegate's confidence and wasting both parties' time
Delegation happens verbally with no written expectations, leading to misalignment and frustration
Owner takes tasks back at the first sign of a mistake, teaching the team that delegation is temporary and conditional
Manager is given responsibility but not authority, creating an impossible position where they are accountable but cannot make decisions
kpis: Owner spending at least 50% of working hours on strategic activities by month six
At least 70% of daily operational decisions made by the manager or shift lead without owner input
Delegation Matrix reviewed and updated quarterly
Zero instances of the owner taking back a delegated task without a structured coaching conversation
Team survey scores on empowerment and trust improving quarter on quarter
constraints: Delegation requires capable delegates — investment in manager development (B083) may need to precede or accompany delegation
The owner must genuinely release control, which requires a psychological shift that takes time
Some tasks genuinely cannot be delegated in a sole-trader business — the framework must be realistic

time_load.L1: 4 hours (time audit, delegation selection, role clarity document)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (delegation matrix, escalation protocol, manager development)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (time tracking, quarterly reviews, strategic planning)

review_protocol: Weekly: owner-manager alignment meeting reviewing delegation effectiveness. Monthly: owner time split tracked and analysed. Quarterly: delegation matrix reviewed and updated. Six-monthly: team survey on empowerment and decision-making authority. Annually: comprehensive review of owner role evolution.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block helps cafe owners clearly separate their strategic role from daily operations, creating a structured delegation framework that empowers the team and frees the owner to work on the business rather than in it.
implementation_risks: The owner's identity is often deeply intertwined with being the best barista or the hardest worker — letting go is emotionally difficult
Delegation without adequate manager development creates risk — these must happen in parallel
In sole-trader businesses, delegation options may be genuinely limited by team size and capability
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate owner burnout, excessive owner working hours, inability to take time off, or business stagnation due to owner being trapped in operations. Critical for business sustainability.
